coredata.dc:title: Superhydrophilic and topography-regulatable surface grafting on PEEK to improve cellular affinity
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bowen
Leng, Junqing
Ouyang, Zhicong
Yang, Zijian
Zhang, Qing
Li, Qingchu
Li, Dichen
Zhao, Huiyu
coredata.dc:description: Polyetheretherketone (PEEK) has been widely used in the preparation of orthopedic implants due to its biological inertness and similar mechanical modulus to natural bone. However, the affinity between biological tissue (bone and soft tissue) and PEEK surface is weak, leading to low osseointegration and an increased risk of inflammation. The situation could be improved by modifying PEEK surface. Surfaces with good hydrophilicity and proper microtopography would promote cellular adhesion and proliferation. This work presented a two-step surface modification method to achieve the effect. Polyacrylic acid (PAA) chains were grafted on PEEK surface by UV irradiation. Then, ethylenediamine (EDA) was added to introduce amino groups and promote the cross-linking of PAA chains. Furthermore, a mathematical model was built to describe and regulate the surface topography growth process semi-quantitatively. The model fits experimental data quite well (adjusted R2 = 0.779). Results showed that the modified PEEK surface obtained superhydrophilicity. It significantly improved the adhesion and proliferation of BMSCs and MFBs by activating the FAK pathway and Rho family GTPase. The cellular affinity performed better when the surface topography was in network structure with holes in about 25 μm depth and 20–50 μm diameter. Good hydrophilicity seems necessary for the FAK pathway activation, but simply improving surface hydrophilicity might not be enough for cellular affinity improvement. Surface topography at micron scale should be a more important cue. This simple surface modification method could be contributed to further study of cell-microtopography interaction and have potential applications in clinical PEEK orthopedic implants.

(b-e) XPS spectra of PAA-EDA-PEEK and Untreated PEEK including (b) full spectrum and high-resolution spectra of (c) C1s, (d) N1s, and (e) O1s. Groups under the green broken line showed strip topography, groups upon the orange broken line showed network topography, and groups between both broken lines showed transition characteristic of both types of topography. (d) Comparisons of SEM images for PAA-PEEK and PAA-EDA-PEEK according to reaction temperature (E3-A3) and irradiation time (C1-C5). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyetheretherketone (PEEK) has been widely used in the preparation of orthopedic implants due to its biological inertness and similar mechanical modulus to natural bone. However, the affinity between biological tissue (bone and soft tissue) and PEEK surface is weak, leading to low osseointegration and an increased risk of inflammation. The situation could be improved by modifying PEEK surface. Surfaces with good hydrophilicity and proper microtopography would promote cellular adhesion and proliferation. This work presented a two-step surface modification method to achieve the effect. Polyacrylic acid (PAA) chains were grafted on PEEK surface by UV irradiation. Then, ethylenediamine (EDA) was added to introduce amino groups and promote the cross-linking of PAA chains. Furthermore, a mathematical model was built to describe and regulate the surface topography growth process semi-quantitatively. The model fits experimental data quite well (adjusted R2 = 0.779). Results showed that the modified PEEK surface obtained superhydrophilicity. It significantly improved the adhesion and proliferation of BMSCs and MFBs by activating the FAK pathway and Rho family GTPase. The cellular affinity performed better when the surface topography was in network structure with holes in about 25 μm depth and 20–50 μm diameter. Good hydrophilicity seems necessary for the FAK pathway activation, but simply improving surface hydrophilicity might not be enough for cellular affinity improvement. Surface topography at micron scale should be a more important cue. This simple surface modification method could be contributed to further study of cell-microtopography interaction and have potential applications in clinical PEEK orthopedic implants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Poly(ether-ether-ketone) (PEEK) is considered as the next generation of artificial bone substitute material and rises great attention. It has great biocompatibility, chemical stability, and ideal mechanical module that is close to the autologous bone. PEEK has now received approval in clinical application by FDA. Based on 3D printing PEEK technology, it has been widely used in bone implants and tissue engineering scaffolds, including dental implants, interbody fusion cages, boneplasty, etc. [1–3]. However, poor performances of PEEK in bone integration and tissue interface bonding have been also reported. It leads to post-implantation problems, such as infection, severe fiber wrapping and fluid accumulation [4]. Though deep reasons have been still discussed so far, a common view attributes the poor bioactivity of PEEK to its poor cellular affinity. Specifically, both cell adhesion and proliferation are low on the PEEK surface [5].

Hydrophilicity is one of the necessary properties for cellular affinitive implants, which has been the consensus in the field [5]. A prevailing explanation is that the hydrophilic surface recruits more serum proteins, providing anchor points and sufficient nutrition for tissue cells. The hydrophilicity improves cell adhesion and proliferation on the surface [6]. Wei et al. also proved that better hydrophilicity brings greater improvement during the early adhesion stage of MSCs [7]. However, surface microtopography, another property, was found to play an even more important role in tissue cell activities. Surface topography with microscale roughness or special structures can significantly affect the spreading area and morphology of cells, regulate gene expression, and even induce osteogenic or adipogenic differentiation [8,9]. Moreover, different topography types, such as porous, grooves and amorphous structures, have different effects on cellular affinity [10,11]. Among such types, porous topography is relatively easy to construct. Appropriate pore size and distribution in micron scale can improve cellular activity, adhesion and proliferation. In general, porous topography with high specific surface area provides more attachment sites for cells [12,13]. Studies have shown that surface microtopography can enhance cell adhesion through the FAK pathway [14]. The reaction between cells and certain topography is still in active discussion, meanwhile, it reaches no consensus that topography with what scale has best improvement for cellular affinity. Nevertheless, microtopography with appropriate features do provide implants with long-term promotion for cell affinity [15,16]. In summary, PEEK without satisfactory hydrophilicity and micro/nano-topography on its surface might contribute to its poor cellular affinity. Therefore, to achieve better tissue integration and healing promotion, an ideal modified PEEK surface should be highly hydrophilic and possess appropriate microtopography.

Several modification methods of PEEK have been reported, among which chemical grafting has been considered as one of the most effective methods due to its stability and efficiency [17]. Grafted layer has strong covalent bonding with PEEK molecules, so that it can stand wear and tear, providing long-term stability [18]. Chemical grafting allows effectively modifying parts with large surface area, since the reaction usually takes place in all area of PEEK surface simultaneously and uniformly [19]. However, only modest improvement has been made through chemical grafting on hydrophilicity because of the limited grafting ratio caused by the chemical inertness of PEEK [19–21]. Though several kinds of rough and porous topographies have been achieved through grafting polymers on PEEK, a regulatable micro/nano-topography of grafted layer has not yet been reported [20–22]. Thus, the promotion of the hydrophilicity and the construction of regulatable microtopography on the PEEK surface are worth paying attention to. In addition to the new grafting process, it would be essential to further improve hydrophilicity and realize topography-regulating by analyzing the quantitative relationship between grafting process conditions and grafted layer growing pattern systematically.

In this paper, we developed a new two-step surface grafting method for PEEK (PAA-EDA grafting, as shown in Fig. 1 ): Acrid acid (AA) was grafted and polymerized on the PEEK surface through a self-initiated radical reaction to form a polyacrylic acid (PAA) foundation. Ethylenediamine (EDA) was used for PAA chain crosslinking and amination through amide reaction. Based on the grafting method, we proposed a possible growth pattern of the polymer grafted layer. Furthermore, the quantitative relationship between process conditions and grafting ratio was fitted with Arrhenius equation to form a simplified model. The correlation between grafting ratio and surface microtopography, including surface roughness, topography and hole size distribution, was revealed, so that the microtopography can be regulated by carefully choosing the process conditions. With optimized process conditions, superhydrophilicity was achieved on PAA-EDA grafted layers with regulatable microtopography. Cytotoxicity, adhesion, and proliferation tests were then conducted with bone mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) and myofibroblast (MFBs) to examine the improvement on cellular affinity for bone and soft tissues. The possible mechanism of microtopography affecting cell behaviors was further discussed by examining the adhesion proteins/genes expression.

PEEK (450 PF, Victrex, Thornton Cleveleys, UK) was made into disk-shaped samples with a diameter of 14 mm and height of 1 mm by Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF) 3D printing system (Shaanxi Jugao-AM Medical Technology Co., Ltd., China). Both sides of the PEEK sample were then ground with #800 and #2000 sandpapers successively. All PEEK samples were ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol and deionized water (10 min for each) before grafting. The grafting process contains two steps: self-initiated grafting step and crosslinking- amination step. Detailed process is presented as follows:

In step I, acrylic acid (AA, > 99 %, Aladdin, China) was diluted with deionized water to 100 mL solution with concentration of 1 mol/L, and NaCl (> 99 %, TianLi, China) was added into the solution as polymerization inhibitor. The concentration of NaCl in the solution was 2 mol/L. PEEK samples were then put into the AA solution. The beaker was placed in a water bath pot (SHJ-A1, JINNAN, China), which was 10 cm under the Ultraviolet light source (350 nm, 50 W, BOFEIDA, China). The self-initiated grafting was carried out in a fully shaded fume hood, and the temperature and radiation time were set according to the experiment design. Then, the PEEK samples were picked out and ultrasonically cleaned with deionized water and ethanol successively and dried in room temperature.

In step II, EDC (Aladdin, > 98 %) and NHS (MACKLIN, > 98 %) were solved in 100 mL PBS solution (pH 5.5–6). Both concentrations of EDC and NHS were 0.5 mol/L in the mixed solution. Grafted PEEK samples were put into the mixed solution for carboxyl activation for 1 h. Then ethylenediamine (EDA, > 98 %, DAMAO, China) was dropped into the solution to start the amination process. The concentration of EDA was 0.5 mol/L in the mixed solution. The beaker was sealed with sealing film (PARAFILM'M', Bemis, USA) during the process to avoid oxidation. The amide reaction was carried out in the water bath pot at 20 °C for 20 h. Finally, the PAA-EDA grafted samples were picked out and ultrasonically cleaned with deionized water and ethanol successively, and dried in room temperature.
Reaction temperature and UV irradiation time in step I were considered as process condition variables. 5 gradients were set for each variable, and the grouping was shown in Table 1 .
Furthermore, these groups were processed with same conditions in step II, and renamed as “group label+” (e.g. C3 for PAA-PEEK would be renamed as C3+ for PAA-EDA-PEEK).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Bruker D8 ADVANCE, Longrun, China) was used to analyze the chemical element composition with the excitation source energy of 1361 eV. The spectral fitting and peak identification were accomplished with Thermo Avantage. Attenuated total reflection Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometer (ART-FTIR, Nicolet iS10, Thermo, USA) was used to characterize the chemical functional group with 16 scans over the range of 1000–4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 4.0 cm−1. Post processing and peak identification of FTIR spectra were accomplished with Omnic.

Laser confocal microscope (LEXT OLS4000, OLYMPUS, Japan) was used to examine the surface topography and surface roughness with the objective lens of MPLAPONLEXT20 under quick scanning mode. Sa was selected to measure the surface roughness, which reflects vertical fluctuation of the surface on average. SEM was used to observe surface microtopography of the samples with the electron energy of 5 kV. Samples were sputter-coated with 2 nm gold before SEM imaging. The SEM images were processed to measure the statistical distribution of hole diameter with Python.
Optical contact angle measuring system (DAS100, KRUSS, Germany) was used to measure the water contact angle by dropping 3 μL water droplet on the surface of the samples in air.

Analytical balance (Precisa, ES225SM-DR, Swiss) was used to measure the mass of samples. 4 samples were measured together each time, and measuring would repeat 10 times. Before mass measurement, the sample was ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol and dried in air under 0.1 atm at 60 °C for 1 h with vacuum drying oven. Then, it was tested under ambient conditions of 25 °C and humidity of 50%RH. Samples in the same group were measured before and after grafting, and the mass increase was considered as the grafting mass.

PAA-EDA-PEEK samples were embedded in castable mounting resins. The longitudinal section of the samples was polished with #400, #800, #1500, #2500, #3000 and #5000 sandpapers in turn. It was observed with SEM (SU-8000, HITACHI, Japan), and the thickness of grafted layers was measured on the SEM images.

PAA-EDA-PEEK samples were immersed in PBS solution (pH = 7.0) with 37 °C water bathing. The mass of samples was measured on day 0, 3, 6, 10, 13 with analytical balance. PEEK samples were treated in the same way as a control group. The degradation ratio of grafted layers was calculated then, and the formula is “degradation ratio = degraded mass/initial mass of grafted layer”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bonding strength between grafted layer and PEEK
ce:section.ce:para: Cuboid samples (8 × 8 × 15 mm) were prepared for bonding strength test. The square face in 8 × 8 mm was grafted by PAA-EDA layer. The face was bonded to un-grafted square face of another cuboid sample. Both faces were aligned and glued together along the central axis with a two-component adhesive (DP420, Scotch-Weld, 3 M, USA) as a tension testing sample shown in Fig. S5. The samples were fixed with a clamp, and the adhesive was cured at 60 °C for 4 h. The prepared tension samples were fixed, and tensile tests were conducted by a biomechanical testing machine (CMT4304, MTS Corp, USA). The force-displacement curves were recorded, and the stress corresponding to the maximum load was defined as the bonding strength between grafted layer and PEEK.

Three kinds of samples were used to evaluate cell affinity: PEEK, E2+, B3+. Their descriptions are as follows: (1) PEEK: pure PEEK samples without any grafting, working as blank control. (2) E2+: PAA-EDA-PEEK samples grafted under 30 °C for 30 min, representing lightly rough grafted layers. (3) B3+: PAA-EDA-PEEK samples grafted under 60 °C for 50 min, representing highly rough grafted layers. Before cell experiment, all samples were autoclaved at 103.4 kPa (1.05 kg/cm2), and maintained at 121.3 °C for 30 min.

The PEEK (n = 4), E2+ (n = 4), and B3+ (n = 4) samples with a diameter of 14 mm and height of 2 mm were put into 24-well plates for cell experiment in vitro. The bone mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs, Cyagen, RASMX-01101) and myofibroblasts (MFBs, iCell, RAB-iCells006) with a density of 1 × 105 cells were seeded on the samples respectively. The same concentration of BMSCs and MFBs were seeded on 24-well plates as control. BMSCs were cultured with α-MEM complete medium (α-MEM basal medium +10 % fetal bovine serum), and MFBs were cultured in DMEM complete medium (DMEM basal medium +10 % fetal bovine serum). After 6 h of culture, the cytotoxicity of the samples was studied by the CCK8 (methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide) assay in accordance with the instructions in the manufacturer's protocol (Sigma-Aldrich, USA). The CCK8 reagent was added to the culture medium, and the cells were incubated in a 37 °C cell incubator for 2 h. Then, the OD value of the culture medium was measured at 450 nm.

After BMSCs/MFBs culturing for 24 and 72 h on three different types of samples (PEEK, E2+, B3+, n = 4), the culture medium was removed, and the samples were cleaned by PBS solution. The cells attached to the sample were digested with trypsin, and then centrifuged and resuspended in 1 mL culture medium. Cell density was counted with a cell counter under the microscope.

At 7 days, adhesion genes (FAK, Talin, and Vinculin), Rho family GTase (RhoA, Rac1 and Cdc42) and cytoskeletal gene (F-actin) were analyzed by RT-PCR analysis, after BMSCs/MFBs had been seeded on each sample in α-MEM/DMEM medium or α-MEM/DMEM medium with FAK inhibitor PF573228 (5 μM). The cells were collected, and total RNA was extracted by TRIzol reagent (Invitrogen), followed by phenol chloroform-isoamyl alcohol extraction. RNA (1000 ng) was placed into a 20 μL reaction system for reverse transcription with the PrimeScript RT Reagent Kit (Takara) according to the manufacturer's instructions. RT-PCR was performed with SYBR Green PCR master mix (Takara) on the RT-PCR System (Applied Biosystems 7500). GAPDH was used as a housekeeping gene. The primer sequences used in this study are listed in Table 2 .

At 7 days, adhesion protein (FAK, Talin, and Vinculin), Rho family GTase (RhoA, Rac1 and Cdc42) and cytoskeletal protein (F-actin) were analyzed by western blot, after BMSCs/MFBs had been seeded on each sample in α-MEM/DMEM medium or α-MEM/DMEM medium with FAK inhibitor PF573228 (5 μM). Cells were collected and treated with RIPA buffer respectively, and the protein content of cell lysates was determined by BCA method. The cell lysate was incubated overnight with primary antibodies at 4 °C: GAPDH (ZSGB-Bio, China), FAK (Proteintech, 12636-1-AP), Talin (Proteintech, 14168-1-AP), Vinculin (SAB, 13104), RhoA (huabio, ET1611-10), Rac1 (SAB, 36742), Cdc42 (Proteintech, 10155-1-AP), F-actin (Abcam, ab130935). This was followed by incubation with secondary antibody for 60 min. Chemiluminescence was measured with an enhanced chemiluminescence system.

After culturing for 72 h, the cells on three types of samples were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde solution for 30 min, and then stained with DAPI and Alexa Fluor 594 phalloidin. The confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Nikon A1R, Nikon Co., Japan) was used to observe the cell morphology. The average area of normal BMSCs/MFBs on confocal culture dishes was recorded as 100 %, and then the degrees of extension of the adherent cells on the three samples were calculated.

For both grafting process experiment and cell experiment, all experiment groups contain 4 samples. For the grafting process experiment, multiple regression analysis was conducted to fit data with Arrhenius equation, and adjusted R-Square was used to examine the model. For cell experiment, the statistical significance of the data was assessed with an analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Bonferroni's multiple comparison test (α = 0.05).

Fig. 2a shows the ATR-FTIR spectra of untreated PEEK, PAA-PEEK and PAA-EDA-PEEK samples. The differences of characteristic absorption peaks between these samples indicate that the grafted layer has completely covered the surface of PEEK substrate. Spectra of group C1, C2 and C3 were presented as typical examples. Groups with short reaction time like C1 showed similar spectra with untreated PEEK (including B1, C1, D1, and E1). Little polymer has been grafted for these groups. The spectra of C2 and D2 had characteristic absorption peaks, which were from both PEEK and PAA-PEEK. It could be the infrared light of FTIR penetrating the thin PAA layer (about several microns, shown in Fig. S3) and detecting both signals from PAA and PEEK. Other groups not mentioned above showed similar spectra with C3 for both PAA-PEEK and PAA-EDA-PEEK.

A typical PEEK FTIR spectrum shown in purple is similar to many studies [23]. Peaks at 1650, 1596, 1487, 1218, 1185 cm−1 were ascribed to the diphenylketone unit, aromatic rings and diphenylester unit for the untreated PEEK. For PAA-PEEK, the wide absorption peak at 2942 cm−1 was assigned to OH stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 1695 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CO [24]. For PAA-EDA-PEEK, absorption peaks at 1633 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CO. It should be the frequency shift of CO stretching vibration due to the amide reaction [25]. The peak at 1537 cm−1 was ascribed to the NH bend bridging in the secondary amine [26]. The strong peak at 1398 cm−1 was corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CN and COO- [27]. The wide peak at 2928 cm −1 was assigned to OH and NH stretching vibrations [28].

Fig. 2(b-e) presents the XPS spectra of PAA-EDA-PEEK (group C3+) and untreated PEEK. The grafted layer was mainly composed of C (67.96 % ± 2.13 %), N (10.70 % ± 1.37 %), and O (21.05 % ± 1.74 %), while no Nitrogen element was in untreated PEEK (Fig. 2b). For C1s spectrum (Fig. 2c), peaks of 286.2 and 288.2 eV were attributed to CN and CO respectively in PAA-EDA-PEEK [27]. The peak of 286.4 for untreated PEEK was attributed to CO. The N1s spectrum of PAA-EDA-PEEK (Fig. 2d) shows peaks of 399.7 and 401.3 eV corresponding to NH and CN, respectively [29]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) reveals peaks of 531.4 eV corresponding to CO and 532.7 eV corresponding to C-OH for PAA-EDA-PEEK, while peaks of 533.4 and 532.1 eV were attributed to CO and CO respectively for untreated PEEK [19].

The water contact angles (CA) were measured to certify the wettability of modified surface. Fig. 3a showed typical CA values of group C3/C3+. The grafting of PAA decreased CA from 73.2° to 40.0°. Moreover, the addition of EDA further reduced CA to 0.0°, so that the superhydrophilic surface was obtained. For PAA-EDA-PEEK, notably, there were dynamic changes of contact angles decreasing from high value (81.5° in average) to low value and then keeping stable. The stable CA values were adopted for wettability evaluation. Fig. S1 presented dynamic changes of water contact angle for PAA-EDA-PEEK samples. For all groups with 10 min irradiation time, CAs were much less than that of PEEK (45.0° for the maximum and 20.7° for the minimum). The values became stable after 10 s. For other groups with longer irradiation time, CAs reached 0.0° within 3.5 s, except group C2 (CA = 24.0°, stable after 5 s) and D2 (CA = 24.0°, stable after 20 s). It implied that irradiation time is a key variable to control the wettability of PAA-EDA-PEEK. Theoretically, to obtain superhydrophilic surface, the roughness factor r (ratio of real surface area to projected surface area) defined by Wenzel equation should satisfy the criterion below [30].
r ≥
1
cos θ
where θ is the CA of the material on a smooth surface (Young's contact angle). The CA of E1 was adopted as an approximate of θ (θ = 31.7°). Groups with 0.0° CAs reached within 3.5 s satisfied the criterion exactly and vice versa (Fig. S2).

Typical topographies of untreated PEEK and all PAA-PEEK groups were presented in Fig. 3b and c respectively. Untreated PEEK surface was smooth without complex topography. PAA-PEEK groups, however, can be divided into three collections according to topography features, which have been highlighted with broken lines. Groups with strip topography were under the green broken line, and groups with network topography were upon the orange broken line. Groups between both broken lines showed transition characteristic of both types of topography, including B2, D3 and E3. With the increasing of irradiation time, topographies transformed from strip to network at a certain temperature.

For groups with strip topography, white grains could be observed in E1 and D1. A groove filled with grains was marked by white arrow in D1, proving the formation of these grains in grooves. Actually, grooves originating from sanding scratches were nearly invisible in micron-scale SEM images. While AA monomers trended to accumulate at surface defects like pits or grooves. And with such accumulation, strips were forming along inner edge of grooves making them be observed, since polymer strips were more visible.

Fig. 3d showed comparisons of SEM images for PAA-PEEK and PAA-EDA-PEEK. Group C3 was taken as a standard to present topography changes with PAA reaction temperature and irradiation time. It is obvious that the complexity of topography increased as reaction temperature and irradiation time increased. Topography of PAA-PEEK was described as an accumulation of ‘lumps’, while PAA-EDA-PEEK tended to stretch ‘lumps’ longer, forming larger holes and more complex network. Some hole diameter was beyond 50 μm. Groups with strip topography did not show obvious topography changes attributed to surface groove constraint.

The grafting ratio of PAA on PEEK surface can be described with the mass per area of PAA layer. Fig. 4a shows relationships between UV irradiation time and PAA mass in different temperature. The standard deviation of PAA mass per area for each group was <10 %. In general, the mass of grafted layer increased with reaction time except group B whose grafted layer mass decreased after 50 min. Slight decrease occurred after 70 min for group A, C, and E. Similar tends could be observed in Fig. 4b, which shows relationships between UV irradiation time and surface roughness (Sa) of PAA-PEEK in different temperature. The surface roughness of all groups increased until 70 min, and after then it increased slightly (group D) or decreased (group A-C, E). Thus, we considered points at 70 min as ‘saturation points’ of step I process. Point for D5 was included as an exception. Actually, white gel would cover AA solution after long time irradiation (≥50 min), which might reduce UV light intensity and saturate the grafted layer growth. The white gel could be PAA polymerized by AA monomers with UV-photo inducing.

A platform period for groups with lower reaction temperature (i.e. group C, D and E), which is 10–30 min for group C and D, 30–50 min for group E, can be observed in both Fig. 4a and b. Interestingly, groups with similar mass and roughness of grafted layer had obviously different topographies (e.g. C1&C2, D1&D2 in Fig. 3b). The topography should be restructured to reach a dynamic equilibrium between chain scission and chain growth. No platform period could be found for group A and B, which might be the effect of high polymer growth ratio in high temperature (60–70 °C) makes the platform period unobserved.

The similar tends between PAA mass-irradiation time and PAA-PEEK surface roughness-irradiation time relationships suggested potential correspondence between PAA mass and PAA-PEEK surface roughness shown in Fig. 4c. Fitting straight line was presented and the correlation coefficient was 0.839 (p < 0.001). To show the distribution of topographical types, we assigned green square to points representing groups with strip topography, yellow circle to transition topography and orange triangle to network topography. Groups with similar topography accumulated together, and three groups of topographies were distributed along the fitting line in turn. It was consistent with SEM images in Fig. 3b.

FTIR spectra (Fig. 2a) and surface roughness (Fig. 4b) of untreated PEEK were in good agreement with PAA-PEEK (including B1, C1, D1 and E1) in strip groups. But the grafted layer mass, however, was much larger than zero for these groups. It suggests that polymer chains did grafted on PEEK surface but were difficult to be detected by other methods. Most of them might accumulate in inner edges of grooves.

For transition groups, the PAA polymer expanded not only along the grooves but in other directions (B2, D3, E3 in Fig. 3b). It should be the weakened constraint of grooves on polymer domain direction due to the increasing of grafted layer thickness. The mean depth of grooves was about 200 nm and ≤350 nm, while the strip topography surface roughness could be up to 2.40 μm. The distribution of polymer domain might still be affected by groove constraint when the grafted layer thickness is 6–12 times that of the groove depth. For network groups, polymer chains without constraints accumulated into lumps piling and connecting with each other and formed networks.

Fig. 4d presented the relationship between surface roughness of PAA-EDA-PEEK and PAA-PEEK. Two types of point distribution could be observed and were marked with blue square and olive diamond respectively. Blue squares distributed along a sloping line and olive diamonds along a horizontal line. It was found that olive diamonds were corresponding to saturation points of PAA-PEEK surface roughness, while blue squares were corresponding to other points denoted as ‘normal point’. Surface roughness of PAA-PEEK and PAA-EDA-PEEK represented by blue squares showed high linear correlation (r = 0.895, p < 0.001). For olive diamonds, the correlation coefficient was 0.139 (p > 0.90) suggesting that groups at saturation points had close surface roughness (about 16 μm) after EDA processing.

Other properties of grafted layer are also crucial for its clinical application including thickness, degradation ratio and bonding strength on PEEK surface. E2+ and B3+ were chosen as representatives for examinations. The grafted layer thickness of E2+ was 1.79 μm and B3+ was 18.09 μm as shown in Fig. S3. It could be observed in B3+ that grafted layer was squeezed by cured resin (marked by white arrow). Thus, the surface roughness could be larger than the measured value of the thickness. It indicates that the whole structure of grafted layer was loose and soft without thick dense base. The degradation ratio become stable after 3 days for B3+ and 6 days for E2+ (Fig. S4). Both stable values were <15 %. It suggests that the grafted layer should have a good stability in vivo environment.

The bonding strengths between grafted layer and PEEK surface were 7.47 ± 1.75 MPa for E2+ and 10.20 ± 1.48 MPa for B3+ (Table S1), which could satisfy basic demand of implants [31]. However, it was 1.72 ± 0.47 MPa for PEEK surfaces without grafting. The grafted layer might improve the bonding strength and elongation at break of adhesive layer as the tensile force-displacement curves shown in Fig. S6. Notably, the tensile broken sections (Fig. S7) suggested that the broken occurred due to low bonding strength between the un-grafted PEEK surface and the adhesive layer. The bonding strength between the grafted layer and PEEK should be larger than measured values.

Sterilization was needed before the cell experiment, so that it is essential to analyze the effect of sterilization. All samples were autoclaved for 30 min. Group C3+ was taken as a representative to compare the characteristic differences between samples before and after sterilization. Both ATR-FTIR spectra possess absorption peaks at 3283 and 3055 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of OH and NH respectively. Peaks at 1635 and 1538 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of CO and the NH bend bridging in the secondary amine, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5a. No significant changes in chemical composition. SEM images (Fig. 5b) suggested that no significant differences in topography. However, the static water contact angle increased to 20.9° after sterilization, and recovered to 0.0° after wetting the sample surface with water and drying in air (Fig. 5c). It should be the effect of swelled topography and maintained water molecules on the wetted surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface topography characteristics of PEEK samples for vitro experiments
ce:section.ce:para: The surface roughness of PEEK, E2+, B3+ samples were 0.243 ± 0.009 μm, 2.314 ± 0.500 μm and 23.663 ± 3.531 μm respectively (n = 3, Fig. 6b), measured by laser confocal microscope. SEM images were captured for groups with grafted layer (Fig. 6a). Both E2+ and B3+ were in network topography. As surface roughness only described vertical fluctuation of the surface, the horizontal size of holes of surface network structure should be measured for further analysis. The holes were extracted as described in the material and methods section, after which the number of holes and their areas were counted. The square root of the hole area was utilized as a simply approximate value of hole diameter. Holes with diameter <5 μm were ignored due to their similar scale with image noise. Fig. 6b showed frequency distribution of hole diameters (n = 3 for each group): for E2+, 26.4 % holes were in 10–20 μm and 0.5 % were in 20–50 μm; for B3+, 32.2 % holes were in 10–20 μm and 22.0 % were in 20–50 μm. Standard deviation for results all above were <6 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of PEEK sample on toxicity and proliferation of BMSCs/MFBs
ce:section.ce:para: BMSCs were co-cultured with PEEK, E2+ and B3+ samples for 6 h, and then incubated with CCK8 reagent for 2 h. The OD values of three groups were not statistically significant compared with the control group. Repeat it above with MFBs, the results are similar to BMSCs. In general, the results showed that all three groups of samples were not cytotoxic (Fig. 6c, d). The cell densities were 1.55 ± 0.17 × 105, 1.91 ± 0.23 × 105 and 2.23 ± 0.31 × 105 cells/mL after BMSCs were cultured on PEEK, E2+ and B3+ for 24 h, respectively. After 72 h culture, the cell densities increased to 2.88 ± 0.23 × 105, 3.47 ± 0.35 × 105 and 4.19 ± 0.28 × 105 cells/mL respectively. The cell density changes of MFBs were similar to BMSCs. (Fig. 6e/f) These results indicate that E2+ promotes cell proliferation than pure PEEK, and B3+ performed better than E2 + .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of PEEK samples on adhesion of BMSCs/MFBs
ce:section.ce:para: The adhesion morphology of BMSCs and MFBs on different samples is shown in Fig. 6i and j. BMSCs cells grown on E2+ and B3+ had better elongation than those grown on PEEK (n = 4). While BMSCs elongation decreased after the addition of FAK inhibitor (PF573228) in E2+ and B3+ groups. The degree of extension was further analyzed quantitatively, and the results were in accord with the adhesion morphology. The extension of B3+ group (82.45 ± 6.05) % was significantly higher than that in E2+ group (62.53 ± 7.83) %. After the addition of the FAK inhibitor, there was no significant difference among E2+ (36.03 ± 3.85) %, B3+ (36.93 ± 4.60) % and PEEK (37.53 ± 4.19) % group. MFBs showed similar results BMSCs (Fig. 6h).

The molecular biology mechanism of adhsion of BMSCs or MFBs was further evaluated by Western blotting and Q-PCR. BMSCs or MFBs were seeded on PEEK, E2+ and B3+ samples for 7 days. Compared with PEEK samples, E2+ and B3+ samples could promote the expression of FAK, Talin, Vinculin, Rho family GTase (RhoA, Rac1 and Cdc42) and F-actin cytoskeleton from the protein level, and B3+ performed better (Fig. 6k, l). The expression of these protein decreased significantly in E2+ and B3+ groups after the addition of FAK inhibitor (PF573228), and there was no significant difference with PEEK group. The results of Q-PCR were in accord with Western blotting (Fig. S8a, Fig. S8b). These results indicate that the introduce of microtopography could enhance the adhesion of cells by activating the FAK pathway. Besides, B3+ promotes cell adhesion better than E2+ through FAK pathway, while FAK inhibitor (PF573228) blocked this change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Grafted process and mathematical model for topography regulation
ce:section.ce:para: We systematically analyzed the effect of UV irradiation time and reaction temperature on grafted layer mass. Furthermore, the relationship between grafted layer mass and surface topography was revealed to regulate grafted layer surface topography. It has been demonstrated that UV irradiation can induce self-initiated surface graft polymerization of PEEK without any photo-initiator [23]. Under UV irradiation, a semi-benzopinacol radical forms and initiates the grafting-from polymerization of feed monomers [32]. Since acrylic monomers can also polymerize under UV irradiation [33], both grafting-to and grafting-from modes might exist concurrently in step I.

According to surface topographies of PAA-PEEK shown in Fig. 3b, the PAA grafting process can be divided into three stages: strip stage, transition stage and network stage. The inherent grooves on PEEK surface at hundred-nanometer scale could constrain the early distribution of grafted layer, while almost no constraint effect was observed on grafted layer with large surface roughness (> 2.40 μm) in the later growth stage. In step II, EDA processing introduced amino groups to the surface, and increased steric hindrance between polymer chains. Polymer lumps were stretched longer, which enlarged the diameter of network holes. It enhanced capillarity action of the grafted layer and made the surface superhydrophilic. Fig. 7a showed an illustration of the whole process. To regulate surface topography of PAA-EDA-PEEK through irradiation time and reaction temperature in step I, we quantified the relationship among irradiation time, reaction temperature and grafted layer mass of PAA-PEEK, and built a model based on Arrhenius equation. The reaction rate can be approximately regarded as a constant in photo-induced grafting reaction. Monomers in liquid phase were grafted to solid interface. Thus, the mass of grafted layer increased linearly with time, as shown in Eq. (1).
(1)
m =
M AA
A s
kt
where m is grafted layer mass, t is irradiation time, M AA is the molar mass of AA, A
s is the area of reaction interface, and k is reaction rate constant. Actually, grafted layer mass and irradiation time do not strictly obey the linear equation presented in Fig. 5a. Therefore, an exponential correction α was introduced, and a modified model was obtained as
(2)
m =
M AA
A s
k
t α
where α should be >0 and <1. Eq. (2) cannot describe the platform period and saturation points in Fig. 4a, which might impact the accuracy of the model. However, it is still a good and simple approximation for mass variation. It was then substituted into Arrhenius equation in integral form to obtain Eq. (3).
(3)
ln m = −
E a
RT
+ α ln t + ln
M AA

A
where R is the molar gas constant, E
a is the activation energy of reaction, T is reaction temperature (unit: degree Kelvin), and A is pre-exponential factor. A multiple linear regression model was built based on Eq. (3), presenting fine explanation between variables (adjusted R2 = 0.775, p < 0.001). The fitting value of parameters are shown in Table 3 .

The activation energy of reaction could be calculated according to Table 1, which is 13.49 kJ/mol. This value was close to the overall activation energy of photo-graft copolymerization for methyl methacrylate (MMA) and ethyl methacrylate (EMA) reported by Trivedi et al., which were 8.10 and 10.49 kJ/mol respectively [34].

Fig. 7b described relationships among grafted layer mass, irradiation time, reaction temperature with surface topography. Obviously, the topography transformed along the fitting curve. Surface roughness could be regulated by adjusting reaction temperature and irradiation time, since there was a linear relationship between grafted layer mass and surface roughness as presented in Fig. 4c. Surface roughness of PAA-EDA-PEEK and PAA-PEEK were highly correlated, and their relationship was fully described in Fig. 4d. Thus, semi-quantitative regulation of surface roughness and qualitative regulation of surface topography could be achieved for PAA-EDA-PEEK.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of grafted layer on cellular adhesion and proliferation
ce:section.ce:para: Through experiments on BMSCs/MFBs, it can be found that network surface topography with superhydrophilicity can nicely promote cell adhesion and proliferation. Both BMSCs and MFBs need to adhere first and then proliferate, which cannot be reversed. Thus, good adhesion is also conducive to proliferation. Our results showed that the grafted layer promotes cell adhesion through the FAK pathway, which is a universal cell-spreading signaling pathway for most cells in the human body [35,36]. The activation of FAK could activate Rho family GTPases (RhoA, Rac1, and Cdc42), positively regulating the formation of cytoskeletal protein F-actin and affecting cell morphology. Similar performances on cell adhesion between PEEK surfaces and grafted surfaces with inhibitor (Fig. 6g-l) indicated that the FAK pathway was not activated for PEEK surface with microtopography (sanding scratches). However, so were grafted surfaces. It seems no effect on cell adhesion for microtopography on PEEK surface. Therefore, we infer that hydrophilicity should be necessary for FAK pathway activation and cell adhesion.

Moreover, we found that group B3+ performed better than others, whose network hole size (diameter and depth) partially fitting the cell size surface. Although both E2+ and B3+ groups had superhydrophilic surfaces, the latter showed higher levels of related proteins expression and cellular affinity. Microtopography should play a more important role in FAK pathway activation and cellular affinity improvement for the surface with better hydrophilicity. In general, the microtopography effects on cellular behavior could be summarized as follows: (1) increasing the specific surface area to provide more cell attachment sites [37,38], and (2) guiding cells to migrate, gather and form solid contact with each other [39,40]. It was reported that osteoblasts would stride over 0.45 μm pit or grew along its border, but intrude inside 2.2 μm pit with pseudopods for potential migration [39]. Another study showed that MSCs would migrate into the pores of around 20 μm [40]. M. Bigerelle et al. [41] also found that large and deep cavities of 10 μm can provide “nest” for cells. In our study, the characteristic holes were in about 2.5 μm depth and 10–20 μm diameter for E2+, and they were in about 25 μm depth and 20–50 μm diameter for B3+. Both groups should be able to induce cell pseudopods to extend inward, but B3+ could further induce BMSCs to migrate into the holes. It seemed that the network holes fitting the cell size range further promoted the cell adhesion since holes with smaller sizes both existed on E2+ and B3+ (Fig. 6b). Fig. 7c showed an illustration of cell spreading morphology on three types of surfaces as a possibility.

At present, it is still inconclusive for the effect of surface topography on cell adhesion and proliferation at micron/nano scales [42–44]. Previous studies have shown that surface wettability mainly affects the early adhesion of cells, while surface topography dominates long-term adhesion and proliferation [44,45]. It has been proved that good wettability could enhance the adsorption of serum proteins, thereby promoting cell adhesion and proliferation [46–48]. For materials with hydrophilic surface, such as aluminum oxide, titanium and its alloys, micro/nano-topography has significant promoting effects on cell adhesion and proliferation [46,49–51]. However, without serum protein, topographic factor could not provide any significant promotion for the adhesion of hMSCs [4]. On the contrary, for materials with hydrophobic surface, like polystyrene, PEEK and Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), micro/nano-topography has no significant positive effects. In some cases, the topography even slightly inhibits proliferation [39,52,53]. Our experimental results showed that the effect of microtopography was limited without good hydrophilicity. In fact, hydrophilicity mainly affects the initial adsorption of serum protein, which is a necessary condition for good cell affinity. However, improving surface hydrophilicity might not be enough for cellular affinity improvement, and surface topography at micron scale could be a more important cue for cell-surface interaction.

It is noticeable that our conclusion is limited to PEEK and PAA-EDA-PEEK, and it was only examined on BMSCs and MFBs. Although the good cellular affinity is attributed to superhydrophilicity and microtopography, the potential effect of chemistry property could not be totally neglected. Furthermore, it needs further research whether other sizes of holes or types of topographies perform better. Nevertheless, the conclusion still has instructional significance for surface modification of PEEK orthopedic implants that hydrophilicity should be necessary for cell adhesion and topographies with better performance could be constructed based on this.

A novel PAA-EDA two-step surface modification method based on photoinduced grafting was proposed to construct micron scale superhydrophilic network topography on PEEK surface, promoting adhesion and proliferation of BMSCs/MFBs. The PEEK surface became superhydrophilic with the effects of the amino groups and network topography. As a result, the water contact angle could reach 0.0° within 3.5 s. Furthermore, a mathematical model was built based on the Arrhenius equation to regulate the topography semi-quantitatively (adjusted R2 = 0.775). By adjusting process conditions, surface topography could present strip, transition, or network structures. In fact, the superhydrophilic surface could active the FAK pathway to improve cell adhesion and proliferation. In addition, it performed better when the network topography of the surface possessed holes in 25 μm depth and 20–50 μm diameter, which might provide cells with more attachment sites than smaller holes. Thus, good hydrophilicity should be necessary but not sufficient for cellular affinity improvement. The topography at micron scale could be a more important cue when the surface is in good hydrophilicity. This research could be instructive for surface modification of PEEK orthopedic implants.
